5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Template use from Canva.comHow Laravel Queues Actually Work Under the Hood
Laravel was created by Taylor Otwell and first released in 2011. Queues have been part of it since early on, originally just a simple wrapper around Beanstalkd, and over the years, it grew to support Redis, Amazon SQS, and a database driver, with chaining, batching, and rate limiting added on top as the framework matured. That queue system is what we’re digging into here.
Every Laravel developer uses dispatch() at some point. You call it, the job runs later, and everything just works. But if you're curious what actually happens in between, this post walks through it step by step, with real code.
Why queues exist
Some tasks are slow: sending an email, resizing an image, calling a third-party API. If you run these directly inside a web request, the user sits there waiting. Queues solve this by pushing the task somewhere else to be handled in the background, so the request finishes instantly.
Step 1: Creating a job
Start with the artisan command:
php artisan make:job ProcessPodcastThis gives you a job class like this:
class ProcessPodcast implements ShouldQueue
{
use Dispatchable, InteractsWithQueue, Queueable, SerializesModels; public $tries = 3;
public $backoff = 10;
public function __construct(public Podcast $podcast) {
}
public function handle(): void
{
$this->podcast->process();
}
public function failed(\Throwable $exception): void
{
// log it, notify someone, whatever makes sense
}
}
The important part here is ShouldQueue. That single interface is what tells Laravel, "don't run this right away, put it on a queue instead." Remove it, and the job just runs synchronously like a normal function call.
Step 2: What happens when you dispatch it
ProcessPodcast::dispatch($podcast);This doesn’t run your handle() method yet. Instead, Laravel does two things:

• Converts your job object into a plain string (serialization), so it can be stored somewhere.

• Hands that string off to whatever queue driver you’re using (database, Redis, SQS, etc).
The serialization part is why SerializesModels is on the job. Instead of storing the entire Podcast model as a blob, Laravel just stores the model's class name and ID. When the job runs later, it fetches a fresh copy from the database. This keeps the stored payload small and avoids storing stale data.
The final payload looks roughly like this:
{
"uuid": "a1b2c3d4",
"displayName": "App\\Jobs\\ProcessPodcast",
"job": "Illuminate\\Queue\\CallQueuedHandler@call",
"data": {
"commandName": "App\\Jobs\\ProcessPodcast",
"command": "O:22:\"App\\Jobs\\ProcessPodcast\":..."
},
"attempts": 0
}That’s it. That JSON string is your job, sitting in storage, waiting for a worker to pick it up.
Step 3: Where it actually lives
Laravel doesn’t care where jobs are stored; it just needs something that can push and pop payloads. That’s the queue driver’s job.

• database — inserts a row into a jobs table

• redis — pushes onto a Redis list

• sqs — sends it to an AWS queue

• sync — doesn’t queue anything, runs immediately (mostly used for local testing)
If you’re using the database driver, dispatching a job is really just this insert happening behind the scenes:
DB::table('jobs')->insert([
'queue' => 'default',
'payload' => $payload,
'attempts' => 0,
'available_at' => now()->timestamp,
'created_at' => now(),
]);Nothing magical, it’s literally a row in a table sitting there until something comes along to process it.
Configuring the driver in .env
Which driver gets used comes from your .env file:
QUEUE_CONNECTION=databaseChange this to redis, sqs, or sync to switch drivers without touching any code. Each driver has its own settings in config/queue.php, but the common ones you'll set in .env are:
# Database driver
QUEUE_CONNECTION=database
DB_CONNECTION=mysql
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_DATABASE=laravel
DB_USERNAME=root
DB_PASSWORD=# Redis driver
QUEUE_CONNECTION=redis
REDIS_HOST=127.0.0.1
REDIS_PASSWORD=null
REDIS_PORT=6379
# SQS driver
QUEUE_CONNECTION=sqs
SQS_KEY=your-key
SQS_SECRET=your-secret
SQS_PREFIX=https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/your-account-id
SQS_QUEUE=default
AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1
If you’re using the database driver, don’t forget to create the jobs table:
php artisan queue:table
php artisan migrateStep 4: The worker
This is the part people usually don’t think about: nothing runs automatically just because you dispatched a job. You need a worker process actually watching the queue.
php artisan queue:workThis command starts a loop that never stops (until you stop it). Each cycle it does the same thing:

• Ask the driver for the next available job

• If there is one, unserialize it back into a real object

• Call handle() on it

• If it succeeds, delete it from storage

• If it throws an exception, decide whether to retry it or mark it as failed
Here’s roughly what that loop looks like internally:
protected function runNextJob($connectionName, $queue, WorkerOptions $options)
{
$job = $this->getNextJob(
$this->manager->connection($connectionName), $queue
); if ($job) {
$this->process($connectionName, $job, $options);
} else {
$this->sleep($options->sleep);
}
}
If there’s no job available, the worker sleeps for a bit and checks again. That’s why it queue:work needs to keep running in the background (usually managed by something like Supervisor in production).
Step 5: Retries and failures
Jobs fail. APIs time out, servers crash mid-process, whatever. Laravel handles this using the $tries and $backoff properties on your job.
When a job throws an exception, the worker checks how many times it’s already been attempted:
if ($job->maxTries() !== 0 && $job->attempts() >= $job->maxTries()) {
$this->failJob($job, $exception);
}If there are attempts left, the job goes back onto the queue with a delay (the $backoff value) before it's tried again. If it's out of attempts, it moves to the failed_jobs table, and your failed() method gets called.
You can look at everything that failed and retry it manually later:
php artisan queue:retry allA few things built on top of this
Once you understand the core loop above, these features make a lot more sense; they’re really just extra logic layered on the same dispatch → store → work pattern.
Chaining runs jobs one after another, and stops if one fails:
Bus::chain([
new ProcessPodcast($podcast),
new OptimizeAudio($podcast),
new NotifySubscribers($podcast),
])->dispatch();Batching runs a group of jobs and lets you react once they’re all done:
Bus::batch([
new ProcessPodcast($podcast1),
new ProcessPodcast($podcast2),
])->then(function ($batch) {
// everything finished
})->dispatch();Wrapping up
At its core, a Laravel queue is just three moving parts: something that dispatches and serializes a job, somewhere to store it, and a worker loop that keeps pulling jobs off that storage and running them. Everything else, retries, chaining, and batching, is built on top of that same simple idea.
Once you see it this way, debugging stuck jobs or choosing the right queue driver no longer feels like guesswork.
